THE BOSTOn GLOBE WEDNESDAY,JANUARY 8,2025BOSTONGLOBE.COM/METRO
Norovirus cases in Mass.surged in2024
Officials say 66% jump in a year reflects  US trend; call handwashing best defense
By Camilo Fonseca GLOBE STAFF
Mirroring a national trend, cases f the norovirus stomach bug are urging in Massachusetts this winer, according to state data.
添司oy-confmd e of e u 024 in preliminary data released at wek  te ts artment of Public Health. That epresents a jump of roughly 66ercent from the previous year,hn the ae pord  s.
Cases over the winter have so far utpaced the previous year, with 34 cases reported in November     , thee e 2 s ported  November and 112 in December.
Public health officials say they have been monitoring the increase in cases.
Nationally, cases of the virus are surging in parts of the United States this winter, according to government data.

The most recent numbers from the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention show there were 91 out breaks of norovirus reported during the week of Dec. 5, up from 69 outbreaks the last week of Novemberr.
Numbers from the past few years  show a maximum of 65 outbreaks reported during that first week of December.

Norovirus symptoms include nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, and abdominal cramps, according to state health off icials. Despite being one of the most common causes of foodborne illness, the virus is difficult to detect in a laboratory, and speciallized norovirus tests are usually conducted only in certain circumstances.

EM A spokesperson for the DPH said the uptick in cases may be attributed in part to more modern testing methods which can detect multiple pathogens at once. They also noted that certain test devices were recently found to be prone to false
positives of norovirus.
Illness caused by norovirus typically starts suddenly, with symptoms developing 12 to 48 hours following exposure to the virus. Most  people get better within one to three days and recover fully.
司a ed and fall sick from norovirus.Young children, older people, and those with weakened immune systems are most at risk, with dehydration from vomiting and diarrhea the top concern.
There is no medication to treat norovirus. Rehydration is recommended by drinking water and other liquids, with the exception of coffee, tea, and alcohol.
Health officials also recommend NOROviRUS, Page B3

Pedestrians donned an assortment of winter gear as they walked in downtown Boston Tuesday
Boston td urwst in nation
Study finds drivers here spent an average 79 hours stuck in traffic last year
By Shannon Larson GLOBE STAFF
In what will come as no surprise any Boston driver: We're sill tuck in trafic. The city continues rank in the top-five most coested cities in the United States nd among the worst worldwide.
Still, Boston traffic is generally mproving. Unless, that is, you're eading south on I-93 after work.

a st for any of the top 25 most conedmr ci

Some key factors, according to the Mass. Department of Transportation, include investments in infrastructure, such as optimized traffic signals and dedicated bus lines;public transit enhancements; more people choosing biking or walking;and folks adjusting their commuting schedules.
公司添"We're encouraged by this progress, but know there's still work to be done," said the state highway administrator, Jonathan Gulliver.
Worldwide, congestion again edged closer to pre-pandemic levels as more than half of the urban ar

eas studied experienced an increase in traffic – a trend driven, in part,by workers returning to the office.Istanbul, Mexico City, London, and Paris were among the most congested cities, according to the study.
添司worker lost 43 hours to traff ic jams,the equivalent of a work week, INRIX found.

"Traffic can be an indicator of economic boon, but ironically, it's a hamper on economies in of itself.Each minute spent waiting in traffic results in money and productivity lost," said Bob Pishue, transpor
tation analyst at INRIX and author of the report, which determined that $74 billion was lost to congestion nationwide.

For another year running, New York was the most congested city in the US, according to the scorecard,with the average New York commuter losing 102 hours, followed by Chicago drivers who also lost 102hours, and Los Angeles motorists,who lost 88 hours. People behind the wheel in Boston came in fourth nationwide, the same position it was ranked by INRIX in 2023.
In Boston, congestion resulted in the average speed of a downtown trip crawling along at 13 miles per hour – the second-slowest in the 公司添

Population  rise Mass. is largest  in 60 years
Census figures show increase largely driven by infl ux of im migrants
By Sam Drysdale STATE HOUSE NEWS

Fueled by the highest immigration levels in decades, Massachusetts saw its largest popula tion increase in 60 years between 2023 and 2024,and the rate of domestic outmigration has signiffi cantly slowed, according to US Census data re leased last month.
Between July 1, 2023 and July 1, 2024,the state's population increased 69,603 from 7,066,568 to 7,136,171– an increase of just un der 1 percent (0.985 percent).[Ta

That's the largest annual percentage increase that Massachusetts has experienced since be tween 2012 and 2013, according to a UMass Do nahue Institute analysis of the December US Cen sus population estimates.
Numbers-wise, it's the largest annual popula tion increase since the end of the baby boom ir 1964.[al
E  Those gains made Massachusetts the fastest growing state in New England and the second-fastest growing state in the Northeast after New Jersey, according to the institute.
The largest driver: immigration, estimated at 90,217incoming immigrants between 2023 and 2024. The
90,217The estimated number of immigrants that came to Massachusetts between 2023and 2024
SOURCE:US Census data
census estimates that's the highest immigratio level since at least 1990.

"While immigration is the single largest drive of population change in Massachusetts .…. othe factors also play a role," according to the insti tute's analysis. "Iin 2024, Massachusetts saw mor births (67,851) than deaths (61,133), contribut ing 6,718, on net, to the population growth i Massachusetts."

Massachusetts has seen an influx of thousand of refugees and migrants — seeking asylum fron wars, political unrest, and environmental disas ters — who have sought temporary housing in th state's emergency family shelter system, strainin the physical and financial limits of the system The census data does not delineate between im migrants legally seeking asylum or refuge an  those otherwise entering the country.

Ea scribe people moving out of one state to anothe state, has ben a contentious political topic fo the t families left Massachusetts in the early days the pandemic, ling i taes with lower costs  of living._。

"There probably isn't one magic bullet for obesity," Dr. Field said in 2018, when she was lead author of a paper published in the journal Obesity that analyzed data from more than 2,400 patients who had undergone bariatric weight-loss surgery.
"If there is aicbull," he add,"it'sigto e diferent for different groups of people."

She was 58 and had survivedcancer three times during he
